title: 第 22 節
en: These first steps which primitive man must have made at a very early time, may seem but small steps, but they were very decisive steps, if you consider in what direction they would lead. They were, the steps that would lead man, whether he liked it or not, from the perception of finite things, which he could handle, to what we call the per ception of things, not altogether finite, which he could neither span with his fingers, nor with the widest circle of his eyes. However small the steps at first, this sensuous contact with the infinite and the unknown, gave the first impulse and the lasting direction in which man was meant to reach the highest point which he can ever reach, the idea of the infinite and the divine.
Semi-tangible objects.
I call this second class of percepts semi-tangible, in order to distinguish them from the first class, which may for our purposes be designated as tangible percepts, or percepts of tangible objects.

This second class is very large, and there is con siderable difference between the various percepts that belong to it. A flower, for instance, or a small tree, might scarcely seem to belong to it, because there is hardly anything in them that cannot become the object of sensuous perception, while there are others in which the hidden far exceeds the manifest or visible portion. If we take the earth, for instance, it is true that we perceive it, we can smell, taste, OBJECTS TANGIBLE, SEMI-TANGIBLE, INTANGIBLE. 179 touch, see and hear it But we can never perceive more than a very small portion of it, and the primi tive man certainly could hardly form a concept of the earth, as a whole. He sees the soil near his dwelling, the grass of a field, a forest, it may be, and a mountain on the horizon ; — that is all. The infinite expanse which lies beyond his horizon he sees only, if we may say so, by not seeing it, or by what is called the mind's eye.

This is no playing with words. It is a statement which we can verify for ourselves. Whenever we look around us from some high mountain peak, our eye travels on from crest to crest, from cloud to cloud. We rest, not because there is nothing more to see, but because our eyes refuse to travel further. It is not by reasoning only, as is generally supposed, that we know that there is an endless view be yond ; — we are actually brought in contact with it, we see and feel it. The very consciousness of the finite power of our perception, gives us the certainty of a world beyond ; in feeling the limit, we also feel what is beyond that limit.

We must not shrink from translating the facts before us into the only language that will do justice to them : we have before us, before our senses, the visible and the tangible infinite. For infinite is not only that which has no limits, but it is to us> and it certainly was to our earliest ancestors, that also of which we cannot perceive the limits.
Intangible objects.
But now let us go on. All these so-called semitangible percepts can still be verified, if need be, by some of our senses. Some portion, at least, of N 2 180 LECTURE IV.
every one of them, can be touched by our hands.

But we now come to a third class of percepts where this too is impossible, where we see or hear objects, but cannot strike them with our hands. What is our attitude towards them \ Strange as it may seem to us that there should be things which we can see, but not touch, the world is really full of them ; and more than that, the primitive savage does not seem to have been very much disturbed by them. The clouds to most people are visible only, not tangible. But even if, particu larly in mountainous countries, we reckoned clouds among the semi-tangible percepts, there is the sky, there are the stars, and the moon, and the sun, none of which can ever be touched. This third class I call non-tangible, or if I might be allowed to coin such a technical term, intangible percepts.

We have thus, by a simple psychological analysis, discovered three classes of things, which we can per ceive with our senses, but which leave in us three very distinct kinds of impression of reality :

(1) Tangible objects, such as stones, shells, bones, and the rest. These were supposed to have been the earliest objects of religious worship by that large school of philosophers who hold fetishism to be the first beginning of all religion, and who maintain that the first impulse to religion came from purely finite objects.
(2) Semi-tangible objects, such as trees, mountains, rivers, the sea, the earth. These objects supply the material for what I should propose to call semideities.

(3) Intangible objects, such as the sky, the stars, OBJECTS TANGIBLE, SEMI-TANGIBLE, INTANGIBLE. 181 the sun, the dawn, the moon. In these we have the germs of what hereafter we shall have to call by the name of deities.
Testimonies of the ancients as to the character of their gods.

Let us first consider some of the statements of ancient writers as to what they considered the char acter of their gods to be. Epicharmos says1, the gods were the winds, water, the earth, the sun, fire, and the stars.

Prodikos2 says that the ancients considered sun and moon, rivers and springs, and in general all that is useful to us, as gods, as the Egyptians the Nile ; and that therefore bread was worshipped as Demeter, wine as Dionysos, water as Poseidon, fire as HephaBstos.
Caesar3, when giving his view of the religion of the Germans, says that they worshipped the sun, the moon, and the fire.
Herodotus4, when speaking of the Persians, says that they sacrificed to the sun, the moon, the earth, fire, water, and the winds.

Celsus5, when speaking of the Persians, says that they sacrificed on hill-tops to Dis, by whom they mean the circle of the sky ; and it matters little, he adds, whether we name this being Dis, or * the Most High,'
1 Stobaeus, ' Floril.' xci. 29. CO nev'ETrlxappos TOVS fcovs flvai Xeyet, 'Avefwvi, v8(op, yrjv, fj\iov, rrvp, dcrrepas.
2 Zeller, ' Philosophic der Griechen,' p. 926. Sext. Math. ix. 1 8, 51 ; Cic. N. D. i, 42, 118; Epiph. Exp. Fid. 1088, C.
s Bell. Gall. vi. 21.
4 Herod, i. 31.
5 Froude, in 'Eraser's Magazine,' 1878, p. 157.

or Zet/V, or Adonai, or Sabaoth, or Ammon, or with the Scythians, Papa/ Quintus Curtius gives the following account of the religion of the Indians : ' Whatever they began to reverence they called gods, particularly the trees, which it is criminal to injure1/ Testimony of the Veda.

Let us now turn to the old hymns of the Veda themselves, in order to see what the religion of the Indians, described to us by Alexander's companions and their successors, really was. To whom are the hymns addressed which have been preserved to us as the most ancient relics of human poetry in the Aryan world \ They are addressed not to stocks or stones, but to rivers, to mountains, to clouds, to the earth, to the sky, to the dawn, to the sun — that is to say, not to tangible objects or so-called fetishes, but to those very objects which we called semi-tangible, or in tangible.

This is indeed an important confirmation, and one that a hundred years ago no one could have looked forward to. For who would then have supposed that we should one day be able to check the state ments of Alexander's historians about India and the Indians, by contemporary evidence, nay by a literature, at least a thousand years older than Alexander's expedition to India I But we can go still further ; for by comparing the language of the Aryans of India with that of the Aryans of Greece, Italy, and the rest of Europe, we can reconstruct some portions of that language which 1 Curtius, lib. viii., c. g. § 34. See Happelt, p. 119.
OBJECTS TANGIBLE, SEMI-TANGIBLE, INTANGIBLE. 183 was spoken before these different members of the Aryan family separated.
Testimony of the undivided Aryan language.
zh: 這些原始人類在極早期必然踏出的最初步伐，看似微不足道，但若考量其引領的方向，實則為決定性的步伐。無論人類是否情願，這些步伐都將引導他——從對可觸摸的有限事物的感知，邁向我們所謂的對並非全然有限之物的感知；這些事物既非其手指所能丈量，亦非其目光最廣闊的圓周所能涵蓋。無論最初步伐多麼微小，這種與無限者及未知者的感官接觸，賦予了最初的衝動與恆久的方向，使人類得以抵達其所能企及的最高點——即無限者與神聖者的理念。

然而，在我們能夠完全理解這些較高階段之前，我們必須先審視那些被稱為「半可觸及物體」（semi-tangible objects）的崇拜對象。這些物體既非完全可觸及，亦非完全不可觸及；它們處於感官與心智之間的中介地帶。例如，樹木——當其被視為某種超越單純物質存在的力量之居所時——便屬於此類。在吠陀（Veda）的頌詩中，我們發現樹木被尊崇為神聖的實體，而這種崇拜並非針對木頭本身，而是針對其中所寓居的、不可見的生命力。同樣地，山脈、河流，乃至某些特定的石頭，若被認為具有某種隱秘的靈性力量，亦可歸入此範疇。這些物體之所以「半可觸及」，在於它們的物理存在是可感知的，但它們所承載的神聖性卻超越了感官的直接把握。這種模糊性正是宗教意識在從具體崇拜過渡到抽象概念時的一個關鍵環節。穆勒（Müller）指出，對這類物體的敬畏，為後來更精緻的神學體系鋪平了道路，因為它訓練人類的心靈去辨識現象背後那不可見的「無限者」（the Infinite）。
我將這第二類知覺稱為半可觸知知覺，以區別於第一類——為便於討論，後者可稱為可觸知知覺，即可觸知物體的知覺。

這第二類範圍非常廣泛，且歸屬於此類的各種感知對象之間存在著相當大的差異。舉例來說，一朵花或一棵小樹，幾乎不應被歸入此類，因為它們之中幾乎沒有任何無法成為感官知覺對象的部分；然而，還有其他對象，其隱藏的部分遠超過顯現或可見的部分。以大地為例，誠然我們能感知它，我們能嗅、能嚐、能觸、能聽、能見它。但我們所能感知的，永遠不過是它極小的一部分；而原始人幾乎不可能形成「大地作為一個整體」的概念。他看見居所附近的土壤、田野的草地、一片森林，或許還有地平線上的山巒——僅此而已。那橫亙於其地平線之外的無限廣袤，他只能——如果我們可以這麼說的話——藉由「看不見它」來看見，或者說是藉由所謂的「心眼」來看見。

這並非玩弄文字，而是一個我們可以親身驗證的陳述。當我們從某座高山頂峰環顧四周時，目光便會從一座山脊移向另一座山脊，從一片雲朵飄向另一片雲朵。我們之所以停歇，並非因為再無景物可觀，而是因為我們的雙眼拒絕再向前眺望。我們知道在視線盡頭之外存在著無盡的景緻，這並非如一般所認為的那樣，僅憑推理而得；——我們實際上與之接觸，我們看見它、感受它。正是對我們感知能力有限性的意識，賦予了我們對界限之外那個世界的確信；在感受界限的同時，我們也感受到了界限之外的存在。
我們絕不能退縮，必須將眼前的事實轉譯為唯一能公正呈現它們的語言：在我們面前，在我們的感官之前，存在著可見且可觸及的無限者。因為「無限」不僅指沒有界限的事物，對我們而言——對我們最早的祖先而言也確實如此——它同時也是我們無法感知其界限的事物。

然而，我們必須追問：在感官世界之外，還有什麼其他事物能為宗教提供起點呢？我認為，這個起點存在於人類心靈對「無限者」（the Infinite）的感知之中——儘管這種感知最初必然是模糊且不確定的。事實上，我甚至認為，「無限者」的觀念——即便只是作為「有限者」的否定——乃是宗教思維的真正基礎。倘若我們能證明，人類心靈必然會觸及某種超越可感世界之界限的事物，那麼我們便掌握了宗教學（the science of religion）的基石。語言科學（the science of language）已然教導我們，所有抽象概念皆源於具體概念；然而，同樣真實的是，人類心靈擁有獨特的能力，能夠超越感官所給予的具體事物，並在否定有限者的過程中，瞥見那無限者。這種對無限者的感知，或許最初僅是對「不可見者」的朦朧意識，但它正是宗教的萌芽。正如我們在吠陀（Veda）頌詩中所見，雅利安人（Aryan）的早期祈禱者仰望蒼穹，呼喚「特尤斯」（Dyaus）——那閃耀者——而後逐漸從中發展出「提婆」（Deva）的概念，即神聖者。在此過程中，我們觀察到一種從具體到抽象的運動，但這並非單純的感官產物，而是心靈主動超越感官界限的結果。因此，宗教的起源並非在於對自然物體的崇拜，而是在於對那隱藏在自然物體背後、並透過它們隱約顯現的「無限者」的直覺。這種直覺，正是所有宗教——無論是多神教（polytheism）、單一神教（henotheism）、輪換主神教（kathenotheism），乃至一神教（monotheism）——的共同根源。
但現在讓我們繼續。所有這些所謂的半可觸知感知，在必要時仍可透過我們的某些感官加以驗證。至少有一部分——
> 「……它們中的每一個，都能被我們的雙手觸及。」

但我們現在來到第三類知覺，在此類中，連這種（觸碰）也不可能——我們看見或聽見物體，卻無法用手觸及它們。我們對它們的態度為何？對我們而言，世上竟有可見而不可觸之物，或許顯得奇怪；然而世界確實充滿此類事物。更甚者，原始野蠻人似乎並未因此感到太多困擾。對多數人來說，雲朵僅是可見而不可觸的。但即便我們（特別是在多山地區）將雲朵歸為半可觸知覺，仍有天空、星辰、月亮與太陽——這些無一能被觸及。我將這第三類稱為不可觸知覺，或者若允許我創造一個技術術語，即「非觸知覺」（intangible percepts）。
我們因此透過簡單的心理學分析，發現了三類事物——我們能用感官感知它們，但它們在我們心中留下了三種截然不同的實在印象。
(1) 有形物體，諸如石頭、貝殼、骨頭及其他此類物品。這些被那個龐大的哲學學派視為最早期的宗教崇拜對象，該學派主張拜物教是一切宗教的開端，並堅持宗教的第一個衝動來自純粹有限的事物。
(2) 半可觸及的物體，例如樹木、山脈、河流、海洋、大地。這些物體為我所謂的「半神」（semideities）提供了素材。
(3) 無形物體，例如天空、星辰、太陽、黎明、月亮。在這些之中，我們已具備日後將稱為神祇的雛形。
讓我們首先審視古代作家對於他們所認為的神祇本質的一些陳述。埃庇卡摩斯（Epicharmos）說道¹，諸神乃是風、水、大地、太陽、火與星辰。
普羅迪科斯（Prodikos）² 指出，古人將日月、河川與泉源，以及一般而言所有對我們有益的事物，都視為神祇，正如埃及人看待尼羅河那樣；因此，麵包被當作狄蜜特（Demeter）崇拜，酒被當作戴歐尼修斯（Dionysos），水被當作波賽頓（Poseidon），火被當作赫淮斯托斯（Hephaistos）。
凱撒在陳述他對日耳曼人宗教的看法時，說道他們崇拜太陽、月亮與火。
希羅多德（Herodotus）在談及波斯人時，說道他們向太陽、月亮、大地、火、水與風獻祭。
塞爾蘇斯（Celsus）在談及波斯人時，指出他們在山頂向迪斯（Dis）獻祭，而迪斯意指天穹；他補充道，無論我們稱此存有為迪斯，或「至高者」（*the Most High*），皆無關緊要。
1 Stobaeus, 'Floril.' xci. 29. 科斯人說，神靈乃是：風、水、地、日、火、星辰。
2 Zeller,『Philosophie der Griechen』，頁 926。Sext. Math. ix. 18, 51；Cic. N. D. i, 42, 118；Epiph. Exp. Fid. 1088, C.
> s Bell. Gall. vi. 21.
4 希羅多德，i. 31。
5 弗魯德（Froude），載於《弗雷澤雜誌》（Eraser's Magazine），1878年，第157頁。
或 Zet/V，或 Adonai，或 Sabaoth，或 Ammon，或與斯基泰人同稱 Papa。昆圖斯‧庫爾提烏斯（Quintus Curtius）對印度人的宗教有如下記述：「凡他們開始崇敬之物，皆稱之為神，尤其是樹木——損傷樹木即屬犯罪。」¹ **吠陀的見證**。
現在，讓我們轉向吠陀本身的古老讚歌，以便看清亞歷山大的同伴及其後繼者所描述給我們的印度宗教，其真實面貌究竟為何。那些被保存下來、作為雅利安世界人類詩歌最古老遺蹟的讚歌，是獻給誰的呢？它們並非獻給木石，而是獻給河流、山巒、雲霧、大地、蒼穹、曙光、太陽——也就是說，並非獻給可觸及的物體或所謂的物神，而是獻給那些我們稱之為半可觸及或不可觸及的對象。

這確實是一項重要的佐證，而且是百年前無人能預見的。因為當時誰能想像，有朝一日我們竟能憑藉當代證據——甚至是一套比亞歷山大遠徵印度至少早一千年的文獻——來檢驗亞歷山大史家關於印度與印度人的記述呢？然而我們還能更進一步：透過比較印度雅利安人的語言與希臘、義大利及歐洲其他地區雅利安人的語言，我們得以重建該語言的若干片段。¹ ¹ Curtius, lib. viii., c. g. § 34. 參見 Happelt, p. 119.
在雅利安語系各分支分離之前，這些詞語早已被使用。
雅利安語言之未分裂狀態的見證。
